next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

The Becker County deputies had made seven trips up and down the stairs. From the kitchen table, Joel counted every one. He'd been planted there since quarter to eight with a mug Paul kept refilling, and on each pass the deputies skipped the third step near the top. Paul had warned them about the creak before Joel even thought to mention it.

Cup four sat half-finished in front of him. Too much coffee on top of no sleep and no food had his hands shaking in short fast bursts, and twice he'd caught himself doing what his clients did in arraignment: sitting very still while people with authority moved through a place that used to feel safe. In twenty years as a public defender in Hennepin County, he'd never once considered that side of the room. Now he knew. It felt like shit.

The great room still smelled like Martin's scotch, the Lagavulin he'd poured too generously last night, because Martin never knew how to pour lightly, host reflex still firing even when people told him to sit down. The draft board stayed on the wall, Martin's picks circled in red Sharpie. Nobody had touched it because touching it would mean deciding what it was now: a record, an artifact, evidence. Morning light came through the A-frame windows in thick gold slabs, postcard light, the wrong light for this.

Tommy stood on the deck with both hands around a coffee mug like it was the only solid thing left. A gray clammy cast had settled over his face, the look Joel had seen on defendants listening to charges. In the kitchen doorway, Nate was explaining the lake house's history to a deputy who...
open_hooks_to_carry: Tommy's guilt about the Viagra — played for dark comedy, not melodrama
Tommy's clumsy tampering: taking the pills, dropping one in the hallway, flushing them
Mallick's arrival audible through the bathroom door — Tommy is trapped with evidence as the detective enters
Tommy's physicality under stress — pacing, gripping things, the coaching-mode body in a civilian crisis

payoffs_to_land.description: The joke from Ch 1 becomes Tommy's nightmare — he believes the Viagra triggered Martin's heart failure

prev_tail_excerpt:
...here another beat. The look he gave Paul was one Paul would spend weeks trying to classify, guilt or its cheap cousin, an expression using guilt as cover for whatever was actually moving behind the eyes. Then Tommy went out the sliding door and Paul heard his voice on the deck, his coaching voice, reporting the death of Martin Kessler to the Becker County 911 dispatcher with the clipped factual cadence of a man who had called in injuries before: name, location, situation, time discovered. No emotion in the delivery. All the emotion in the hands that Paul could see through the glass, gripping the deck railing until the knuckles went white.

Paul looked around the kitchen. The fluorescent tube buzzed. Morning came through the pines in thin gold lines that hit the lake and turned the gray to gold that had no right to be beautiful and was.

The sun cleared the tree line. The lake turned from gray to gold. Paul put his cold coffee in the sink and rinsed the mug and set it on the drying rack the way Martin would have wanted, because Martin was particular about his drying rack. That was what finally broke Paul. Not the death. Not the CPR. Not the sounds in the night. The drying rack, the stupid wooden drying rack that Martin had bought at a kitchen supply store in Fargo and been unreasonably proud of. Paul stood at the sink with water running over his hands and cried without making a sound while the sun hit the lake and Tommy's voice carried through the glass door, talking to the dispatcher, giving directions to a house that none of them would ever come back to the same way again.
secondary_character_beats: At least one moment where Tommy's coaching instincts surface inappropriately — he tries to manage the crisis like a halftime talk and it fails
state_deltas_required: Tommy's conviction that the Viagra he gave Martin contributed to the cardiac arrest
Tommy has destroyed evidence linking him to a death he didn't cause, and the detective is now in the house
